params.text:
The five statutory principles

Everything in the MCA flows from five core principles. They are not optional guidance — they are set out in section 1 of the Act and must underpin every decision made under it. Learn them, because they are the backbone of good, lawful practice.

  1. Presumption of capacity — assume a person can make the decision unless it is established that they cannot.
  2. Support to make decisions — a person must be given all practicable help before anyone concludes they cannot decide.
  3. Unwise decisions — a person is not to be treated as unable to decide merely because they make a choice others consider unwise or eccentric.
  4. Best interests — any act or decision made for someone who lacks capacity must be in their best interests.
  5. Least restrictive option — before acting, consider whether the same aim can be achieved in a way that interferes less with the person’s rights and freedom of action.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Each card names one of the five statutory principles. Flip it to see what it means for your everyday practice.

dialogs.answer: Start from the assumption that the person can make the decision. The burden is on proving they cannot — never the other way around.
Try everything reasonable to help first: simple language, pictures, an interpreter, choosing the best time of day, or involving someone the person trusts.
Choosing to eat unhealthily, spend freely or refuse advice is not evidence of lacking capacity. Capable adults are allowed to make choices you disagree with.
If a person genuinely cannot decide, whatever is done for them must be in their best interests — not what is easiest or cheapest for the service.
Always look for the option that limits the person’s freedoms and rights the least, while still keeping them safe and meeting their needs.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap that completes the statement of the five statutory principles.
params.textField: A person must be *assumed* to have capacity unless it is shown otherwise. They must be given all practicable *support* to decide before being judged unable. Making an *unwise* choice does not by itself prove a lack of capacity. Any decision made for someone who cannot decide must be in their *best* interests, using the *least* restrictive option available.
